skill.name: find-skills
skill.description: Discover, vet, and install agent skills by searching ACROSS every major registry at once — skills.sh, clawhub.ai, and GitHub — presenting each board on its own native metric (installs / stars) with the top entry per board, security-scanning the top candidates' real SKILL.md for risky patterns, and flagging what's already installed. Use when the user asks "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that…", "what skill should I install for…", or wants to extend the agent with a capability that might already exist as a published skill. Unlike single-registry search, this surfaces the best of every platform side by side, so you recommend the genuinely relevant, popular, well-maintained, and SAFE one — not whatever ranked first on one site.
skill.summary: Search every agent-skill registry at once and recommend the best, safest match with built-in security scanning.
skill.author: agentspace-so

security_audit.summary: After evaluating all 220 static findings, the vast majority are false positives. The CRITICAL 'dangerous combination' heuristic fires because the script has external_commands, network, and env_access patterns — but the env_access hits are grep PATTERNS inside the built-in security scanner (line 164 checks if OTHER skills reference ~/.ssh, ~/.aws, .env, or private keys), not actual credential reads. All network calls are read-only GETs to public registries (skills.sh, clawhub.ai, raw.githubusercontent.com). The 'weak crypto' hits are false positives from keywords like 'hash' and 'key' in scanner regexes. This is a well-designed, defensive discovery tool with no malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Security scanner references sensitive path patterns
medium_findings.description: Line 164 in scripts/find.sh contains a grep pattern matching '~/.ssh', '~/.aws', '/.env', 'id_rsa', 'AWS_SECRET', 'PRIVATE_KEY'. This is the built-in security scanner that DETECTS if other skills reference these — it does NOT read or transmit any user credentials. False positive: pattern matching, not credential access.

medium_findings.title: Shell command substitution patterns
medium_findings.description: The script uses $(...) command substitution extensively (e.g., $(now_ms), $(wc -l ...), $(date +...)). These are standard shell scripting patterns. User input is safely URL-encoded via jq before being used in URLs, eliminating command injection risk. No unquoted user input is passed to eval or similar dangerous constructs.

low_findings.title: Reads installed skills directories
low_findings.description: The script reads ~/.agents/skills and ~/.claude/skills to detect already-installed skills. This is the explicitly documented purpose of the tool and is read-only.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded registry API URLs
low_findings.description: URLs to skills.sh, clawhub.ai, and raw.githubusercontent.com are hardcoded. This is intentional — the tool queries these specific registries by design.

content.user_title: Find the best agent skill from any registry
content.value_statement: Searching one skill registry gives a biased answer — the #1 result on one site may have 50 installs while a 1,300-install equivalent sits unranked on another. This skill queries skills.sh, clawhub.ai, and GitHub in parallel, ranks each by its own native metric, and security-scans top candidates so you recommend the genuinely best match.
actual_capabilities: Searches skills.sh, clawhub.ai, and GitHub simultaneously in parallel
Ranks each registry by its own native metric (installs for skills.sh/clawhub, stars+match for GitHub)
Security-scans top K candidates' actual SKILL.md for risky patterns (curl-pipe-install, eval-remote, secret references)
Detects already-installed skills from ~/.agents/skills and ~/.claude/skills directories
Flags cross-posted skills present on multiple registries with normalized name matching
Outputs both human-readable formatted report and machine-readable JSON
limitations: GitHub section requires the gh CLI to be installed (degrades gracefully if absent)
Security scan is a heuristic pattern check, not a full security audit
Cross-registry deduplication uses normalized name matching — renamed slugs can be missed
Registry APIs may rate-limit or change; results depend on each board's availability

use_cases.title: Discovering existing skills before building from scratch
use_cases.description: Before spending time implementing a common capability, check if a published skill already covers it across all major registries.

use_cases.title: Vetting third-party skills for safety
use_cases.description: Use the built-in security scanner to identify skills with dangerous patterns like curl-pipe-install or secret file access before recommending them.

use_cases.title: Finding the best-maintained skill for a specific need
use_cases.description: Compare install counts, stars, and maintenance signals across registries to find well-supported skills for deployment, testing, documentation, or domain workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Basic skill discovery
prompt_templates.prompt: Find a skill for React performance optimization
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick search for a common development need

prompt_templates.title: Targeted search with scan limit
prompt_templates.prompt: Find skills for PDF form filling, show me the top 8 results and scan 5 for security
prompt_templates.scenario: Focused search with deeper security vetting

prompt_templates.title: Multi-term adjacent vocabulary search
prompt_templates.prompt: Run adjacent searches for 'frontend design', 'design system', and 'tailwind shadcn' to find the best UI/UX skill

prompt_templates.title: Machine-readable output for automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Find skills for video generation and output as JSON with no security scan

output_examples.input: Find a skill for react performance
output: ALREADY ON YOUR MACHINE: react-perf-toolkit ✓ INSTALLED
skills.sh results: [1] react-perf-toolkit ✓ INSTALLED — 12,400 installs · ✓ clean

output_examples.input: Find skills for pdf form filling --scan 3

best_practices: Run 2-3 adjacent-term searches before concluding — registries index by name, not meaning, so 'frontend design' may surface a 443k-install skill that 'ui ux design' misses
Always read the actual SKILL.md of the top 2-3 candidates before recommending — metadata alone can be misleading
Never recommend a ⛔ RISKY skill without explicitly warning the user about the specific dangerous patterns detected
anti_patterns: Don't trust a single registry's ranking — a skill can rank #1 on one site with 50 installs while a 1,300-install equivalent sits unranked on another
Don't treat the security scan badge as a guarantee — it is a heuristic pattern check, not a full security audit
Don't recommend from match% alone — a 100%-keyword-match repo may be off-topic once you read it

faq.question: What registries does this skill search?
faq.answer: It searches skills.sh, clawhub.ai, and GitHub (topic-tagged repos: claude-skills, agent-skills, claude-code-skills) in parallel.

faq.question: Does it require API keys?
faq.answer: No. It uses read-only HTTP GETs to public APIs. The gh CLI is optional for the GitHub section and degrades gracefully if absent.

faq.question: How does the security scan work?
faq.answer: It fetches the top K candidates' actual SKILL.md files and greps for risky patterns: curl-pipe-install, eval-remote, base64-pipe-exec, broad-tool-grant, reads-secrets, and solicits-credentials.

faq.question: Is the security scan a full audit?
faq.answer: No. It is a heuristic pattern check that catches common dangerous patterns. A clean badge does not guarantee safety — always read the skill yourself before recommending it.

faq.question: Does it auto-install skills?
faq.answer: No. It only discovers and reports. Installation is always an explicit, user-confirmed step via the Skills CLI or clawhub CLI.

faq.question: What does it send to external services?
faq.answer: Only the user's query string (URL-encoded) to public registry APIs. It sends no credentials, tokens, or personal data.
